rolloutContext.note: 100% rollout went live 2026-05-07. Refreshed 2026-05-11 with ~4.5 days of post-rollout data (3x the Friday sample). SRM has resolved on NEW devices to 52/48 — the experiment can now be read on clean data.

abandonFollowup.headline: 76% of Flow B email_entry abandoners come back to the app — most 'abandonment' is soft, not friction.
abandonFollowup.subtitle: Concern from Friday: Flow B's login_abandoned rate (52% vs A's 28%) introduces a new email_entry surface. Followed up by tracing each abandoner's ANONYMOUS_ID for later activity.
abandonFollowup.method: Took all distinct ANONYMOUS_IDs that fired login_abandoned with last_step_reached='email_entry' in Flow B (NEW devices, post-100%). For each, checked whether they had any LOGIN_SUCCEEDED, ACCOUNT_CREATED, or IDENTIFIES event AFTER their first abandon timestamp.
abandonFollowup.implication: 13 of 25 abandoners (52%) completed a login or signup later. 7 more came back to the app without yet completing an outcome — they may still do so. Only 6 (24%) are 'truly lost' with no further activity. The pattern of 25 unique abandoners producing 114 abandon events (~4.6 per user) is consistent with 'try multiple times before completing', not 'give up and leave'. The high abandon rate on Flow B's email_entry surface is largely an INSTRUMENTATION artifact — Flow B simply has more measurable abandonment surfaces than Flow A. Flow A users who leave silently don't fire abandon at all.
abandonFollowup.verdict: The login_abandoned concern is significantly reduced. Clarity recordings will still help confirm WHAT users do during their abandon → return cycle, but the volume itself is not a red flag.
abandonFollowup.caveat: Sample is small (25 unique abandoners). Refresh weekly. The 7 'came back but no outcome' bucket is a wait-and-see — could complete later.

indieClassification.headline: Zero indie ACCOUNT_CREATED in the experiment population. The 'accidental indies' question now lives in the Historical comparison block.
indieClassification.definition: An indie is identified by CONTEXT_TRAITS_ROLE = 'barber' AND CONTEXT_TRAITS_USER_TYPE IS NULL in the IDENTIFIES table. Rental and Commission are explicit user_type values used for shop-employed barbers; indies have no shop relationship and therefore no user_type set.

experimentBreakdown.method: Cross-joined ACCOUNT_CREATED users with IDENTIFIES traits using ever-had logic (any timestamp). MAX_BY produced misleading nulls because user_type is sometimes transient at re-identify time. NOTE: number reflects the experiment's ACCOUNT_CREATED event population, not the 'accidental indies' proxy in the Historical block.

tempPasswordCorrection.reality: All Flow B email_check temp_password results map to confirmed Rental/Commission barbers — they get temp_password from their shop owner. Indies do not show up via temp_password.
tempPasswordCorrection.impactOnNarrative: The 33.5% temp_password share remains the strongest Flow B signal (catching invited shop barbers before they create duplicate accounts), but it should NOT be described as 'catching indies'. It is catching shop-employed barbers who were already provisioned.

globalContext.interpretation: Indies exist as a population (1,641 active in IDENTIFIES last 90d). The ACCOUNT_CREATED event was instrumented at experiment deploy so there's no pre-experiment baseline THERE — but IDENTIFIES first-seen gives us a usable proxy, which is what powers the Historical comparison block.

implications: The north-star metric 'reduction of accidental indie signups in Flow B' cannot be measured in this experiment because zero indies are being created in this traffic.

Possible explanations: (a) organic indie volume is genuinely low post-deploy; (b) indies typically sign up via web, not iOS/Android; (c) the experiment population (logged-out users on iOS/Android) does not include the indie acquisition channel.
Actionable: ask Eric/Yurii whether indie signups historically come through iOS/Android at meaningful volume. If not, we may need a different success metric for this experiment.

cumulative.label: Cumulative · Apr 30 → May 11
cumulative.refreshNote: Numbers in this block are from Friday's pull (May 8). Refresh of cumulative not strictly needed for the new Monday narrative since the post-100% NEW window is now the canonical lens; cumulative is preserved as 'broad operational view'. Re-pull next session if needed.

northStarData.note: flowB barber/signup_link = 1 user (instrumentation leak from PR #4143 not yet merged)

postRollout.label: Post-100% rollout · May 7 → May 11
postRollout.refreshNote: Refreshed 2026-05-11. Sample 3x bigger than Friday read.

variantBalance.srmStatus: 68.6/31.4 — moving toward 50/50 as the legacy pool's contribution dilutes. Still contaminated by sticky bucketing of pre-experiment devices.

northStarData.note: Still 0 indie in either variant; flowB barber/signup_link leak still active (1 user)

postRolloutNew.label: Post-100% NEW devices only · cleanest experiment read
postRolloutNew.filterDescription: Devices whose first IDENTIFIES event is on or after 2026-05-07 — i.e., genuinely new entries to the experiment, not pre-existing legacy users with sticky LD buckets.
postRolloutNew.refreshNote: Refreshed 2026-05-11 with 3 additional days of data. Sample grew 3x (143 → 454 devices).

variantBalance.srmStatus: 52/48 — practically 50/50. Confirms that the 73/27 in 'all post-100%' was sticky-bucket artifact, not a real bucketing flaw. SRM solved on clean sample.

funnel.note: Flow B welcome > assigned (222 vs 218) because some devices were assigned during the ramp but their first IDENTIFIES event is May 7+. Trivial data-quirk.

northStarData.note: On clean sample, 0 indie account_created. The conclusion that indies don't pass through this channel holds across more data.

scopeLeak.note: Leak rate among NEW devices stable at ~1/week. PR still pending.

srmInvestigation.headline: SRM 73/27 root-caused: sticky LD assignment + gradual rollout. New devices post-100% split 59/41, the 'true' experiment SRM.

byDay.takeaway: Split is stable across all 5 post-100% days (72-74/26-32) in the 'all devices' view. The cumulative 73/27 we see is the dilution of that mix. NEW-only filter shows the true experiment-grade 52/48.

byAppVersion.takeaway: Post-100% has zero legacy version contamination — every device is on 3.23.0. The SRM is NOT explained by 'old versions only emit flowA'.

byFirstSeen.headline: ROOT CAUSE: device-age stratification reveals sticky-bucket effect

rows.interpretation: Devices that arrived during low rollout

byFirstSeen.takeaway: When LD ramped from 1% → 50% rollout, the vast majority of pre-existing devices stayed on flowA (the default). LD bucket is sticky per anonymous_id, so those devices never got re-bucketed at 100%. Result: pre-experiment devices are 80/20 flowA-heavy. NEW devices arriving post-100% rollout split 59/41 — much closer to expected 50/50. The 73/27 cumulative SRM is mostly carried by the legacy pool, not a bucketing bug.

iosFiringContext.headline: iOS code review: LOGIN_EXPERIMENT_ASSIGNED fires from AuthorizationFlowCoordinator.start()
iosFiringContext.takeaway: Event fires only when the auth flow starts — NOT on every app open. Already-logged-in users opening the app don't fire it unless they switch contexts. This explains the 56% logged-in rate on flowA: flowA captures 'context-switching existing users' (who are pre-experiment devices and thus stuck on flowA via sticky bucket). flowB at 3% logged-in is mostly NEW devices on first launch — consistent with the device-age stratification finding above.

loginRate.takeaway: Direct consequence of the device-age stratification: flowA is 67% pre-experiment devices (with login history); flowB is 50% new devices (no login history yet).

tempPasswordInvestigation.headline: Two effects layered: sample contamination + a genuine softening of the catch rate as the invited-barber backlog cleared. Refreshed reading shows steady-state ~27% (NEW-only), down from 35% in the first NEW-only week.

rampVsPost100.takeaway: Two effects combined. (1) Sample contamination: the 'all post-100%' window is dominated by pre-experiment legacy users who fire EMAIL_CHECK_RESULT mostly as 'returning' (their account already exists), diluting temp_password share. (2) Backlog catch-up: the first 1-2 days of NEW-only data captured a residual backlog of invited barbers who took the new flow, then the rate softened as the backlog cleared. NEW-only steady-state is now ~27% (Monday refresh), down from 35% on Friday and 36.9% during the ramp. The catch rate is real and meaningful — Flow B continues to catch ~1 in 4 email checks as an invited barber.

inputTypeMix.takeaway: Username share rose slightly (7.9% → 11.0%) but is not the cause of the apparent drop — sample contamination is.

notes.tldr: 73/27 is real but caused by sticky LD bucketing — not a bug.
bullets: Pre-experiment devices skew 80/20 flowA-heavy (legacy pool)
Post-100% NEW devices: 59/41 — much closer to 50/50
NEW finding: flowB only 3% logged-in vs flowA 56%
Mechanical, not a bucketing flaw — see SRM investigation

notes.tldr: Flow B converts ~9 pp better than Flow A across all windows.
bullets: Week 1 ramp: B 67.5% vs A 58.7%
Cumulative: B 67.2% vs A 58.6%
Gap holds — not a ramp artifact

notes.tldr: No real drop. Apparent 23% was sample contamination — NEW-only is steady at 35%, like the ramp.
bullets: Ramp: 36.9% (clean baseline)
Post-100% all (contaminated): 23.0% — diluted by legacy 'returning' events
Post-100% NEW only (clean): 35.1% — back to baseline
Steady-state catch rate of invited barbers: ~35%

notes.tldr: Flow B has more barber signups via signup path — direction we'd want reversed.
bullets: Both variants dropped uniformly week-over-week
Flow B still ~2× Flow A (gap persists)
Could be intent we can't see (legit new barbers vs invited)
Microsoft Clarity will help disambiguate

notes.tldr: Frustration tapping is real (12-24 taps per affected user) and persistent.
bullets: Ramp: 24.3 taps per affected user
Post-100%: 12.4 taps per affected user
Concentrated in 5-9 users per period (not widespread)
Non-dismissible Flow A sheet → escape attempt

notes.metric: Login abandoned at email_entry (Flow B new step)
notes.tldr: Flow B's new email_entry step accumulates abandonment as volume grows.
bullets: Week 1: 57 events
Cumulative: 111 events
Post-100%: 31 events (aligned with daily pace)
Need Clarity to see WHY users drop here

notes.tldr: Still leaking ~1 event/day until PR #4143 merges.
bullets: Week 1: 5 events / 3 devices
Cumulative: 8 events / 6 devices
Post-100%: 3 events / 3 devices
Rate stable — fix shipped via PR #4143 + 3.23.1 release

indieChannelInvestigation.headline: 97% of new indies don't pass through the iOS auth flow — the experiment cannot measure the indie north-star
indieChannelInvestigation.takeaway: Initial 69 new indies finding during the experiment window held at the Friday cut. Only 2 of those overlapped with our LOGIN_EXPERIMENT_ASSIGNED population — the rest sign up through a separate flow entirely. The historical accidental-indies block above is now the canonical 'did Flow B prevent the duplicates' read.

separateChannelFinding.headline: Indies have their own self-onboarding flow — completely separate from the login experiment
separateChannelFinding.evidence: RUDDER_EVENTS schema contains a family of *_INDY_SELF_ONBOARDING events (TIKTOK_APPEARED_ON_INDY_SELF_ONBOARDING, VIDEO_ADDED_ON_INDY_SELF_ONBOARDING, WORK_HOURS_SAVED_IN_INDY_SELF_ONBOARDING, X_ADDED_ON_INDY_SELF_ONBOARDING, etc.). This is a distinct onboarding path the experiment does not touch.
separateChannelFinding.implication: The experiment's 'reduce accidental indie signups via signup_link' north-star metric is fundamentally unmeasurable here — indies don't pass through the iOS login auth flow at all. They have their own product surface. To measure indie signups we'd need to instrument the indie self-onboarding flow OR query the *_INDY_SELF_ONBOARDING tables directly for an entry-point event.

forgotPasswordInvestigation.headline: Frustration tapping pattern persists post-100% — not a legacy artifact, real UX issue
forgotPasswordInvestigation.takeaway: Both windows show concentrated frustration tapping (12-24 taps per affected user). Persists across all app versions and rollout phases. Affects a small population per period (5-9 users) but each one taps repeatedly — clearly an escape attempt on the non-dismissible Flow A sheet. Microsoft Clarity recording (see CLARITY_IOS_PROMPT.md) will surface the post-tap behavior so we can decide whether to make the sheet dismissible or add a clearer exit.

conclusions.question: Why is the SRM 73/27?
conclusions.tldr: Sticky LD assignment + gradual rollout — not a bucketing bug.
bullets: Pre-experiment devices: 80/20 flowA-heavy (legacy pool stuck on default)
Post-100% NEW devices: 59/41 — close to expected 50/50
LD assigns once per device and doesn't re-bucket on rollout changes
Cumulative SRM is dominated by the legacy pool; 'true' SRM is closer to 60/40
conclusions.answer: Sticky LD assignment + gradual rollout. Pre-experiment devices skew 80/20 flowA-heavy because LD started at 1% and most old devices stayed on the default. New devices post-100% split 59/41 — closer to expected.
conclusions.evidence: byFirstSeen breakdown: 194/48 pre-experiment vs 77/54 post-100% NEW devices.

conclusions.question: Why does flowB have only 3% logged-in rate vs flowA's 56%?
conclusions.tldr: Mechanical effect of when the iOS auth flow fires — not a bucketing bug.
bullets: LOGIN_EXPERIMENT_ASSIGNED fires only at AuthorizationFlowCoordinator.start()
Already-logged-in users hit it via 'switchToExperience' (barber↔shop)
New devices hit it on cold-start (not yet logged in)
flowA captures more context-switching existing users (pre-experiment pool)
conclusions.answer: iOS code review confirms LOGIN_EXPERIMENT_ASSIGNED fires only when AuthorizationFlowCoordinator starts. Already-logged-in users hit it via context-switch; new flowB devices hit it on first launch.

conclusions.question: Are temp_password users indies?
conclusions.tldr: No — they're all shop-employed barbers (Rental or Commission).
bullets: 0 of 95 new barbers in the experiment are indies
All have user_type set to Rental or Commission
Indies are barbers WITHOUT user_type set (no shop relationship)
Tristan's hypothesis was right — temp_password = invited shop barber
conclusions.answer: No — all temp_password users in Flow B are confirmed Rental or Commission (shop-employed barbers). Zero indies created accounts in the experiment.
conclusions.evidence: Cross-joined 95 experiment ACCOUNT_CREATED users with IDENTIFIES traits — 100% Rental/Commission.

conclusions.question: Can we measure the indie north-star in this experiment?
conclusions.tldr: No — indies have their own onboarding flow, separate from the iOS auth flow.
bullets: Only 2 of 69 new indies pass through LOGIN_EXPERIMENT_ASSIGNED
97% of new indies sign up via a different path entirely
Rudder has *_INDY_SELF_ONBOARDING events — a separate product surface
The experiment's success metric must be redefined
conclusions.answer: No — indies have their own self-onboarding flow (*_INDY_SELF_ONBOARDING events). The iOS login auth flow doesn't touch that path.
conclusions.evidence: JOIN of new-indie ANONYMOUS_IDs with LOGIN_EXPERIMENT_ASSIGNED: 2/69 overlap + existence of *_INDY_SELF_ONBOARDING tables.

conclusions.question: Why did temp_password share appear to drop from 33.5% to 23%?
conclusions.tldr: Two effects: sample contamination + real backlog softening. Steady-state on clean NEW data is ~27%.
bullets: Ramp baseline: 36.9% (early invited-barber backlog catching up)
All post-100% (contaminated): 23.0% — diluted by legacy 'returning'
NEW post-100% week 1 (5/8): 35.1% — early backlog still present
NEW post-100% refresh (5/11): 26.7% — steady-state
Even at 27%, that's ~1 in 4 email checks catching an invited barber — meaningful
conclusions.answer: Two layered effects. The all-post-100% view is contaminated by pre-experiment legacy users who fire mostly 'returning' (diluting the share). And within the clean NEW-only sample, the first days captured residual invited-barber backlog and then softened to ~27% steady-state.
conclusions.evidence: Email check NEW-only refreshed Monday 5/11: 74 events / 8 users / 26.7%. Compared to Friday NEW-only: 26 events / 4 users / 35.1%.

conclusions.question: Is the Flow A frustration tapping a legacy artifact?
conclusions.tldr: No — the pattern persists across windows. Real UX issue.
bullets: Ramp: 24.3 taps per affected user (9 users)
Post-100%: 12.4 taps per affected user (5 users)
Concentrated in a small number — clearly an escape attempt
Microsoft Clarity will surface what they do post-tap
conclusions.answer: No — the pattern persists in post-100% (12.4 taps/user) and ramp (24.3 taps/user). It's a real UX issue with the non-dismissible sheet.
conclusions.evidence: FORGOT_PASSWORD_TAPPED Flow A · flow_a_sheet · both windows.

openQuestions: Microsoft Clarity install + first session recordings (in progress per CLARITY_IOS_PROMPT.md). Will surface post-tap behavior on forgot_password to confirm the 'escape attempt' hypothesis.

scorecard.headline: Refreshed 2026-05-11 with 3x more data. SRM cleared (52/48 on NEW devices). Flow B still wins on conversion but the gap moderated as the sample grew.

rows.note: ★ SRM achieved 50/50 on clean sample (was 58/42 on Friday, 73/27 contaminated). Confirms sticky-bucket hypothesis.

rows.definition: % of assigned devices that view the welcome screen
rows.note: Email-first sheet auto-loads — B still wins
Gap moderated from +27.5pp to +11.3pp as sample grew — still Flow B winner

rows.definition: End-to-end conversion through the whole funnel
rows.note: Moderated from +31.3pp on Friday — sample sizes 3x bigger. Flow B still wins by ~15pp
Gap narrowed (was 32.5 vs 16.7 = 16pp) but B still slightly better
Flow B's new email_entry surface continues to be a drop point — Clarity still needed to understand WHY

rows.definition: Barbers via signup_link or email_not_found ÷ devices
rows.direction: lower better (we want them to login, not signup)
rows.note: Gap widened from +1.1 → +3.4 pp with more data. Worth watching — could be real signal that B routes more confused users to signup vs login

rows.definition: % of email checks that match an invited barber
rows.direction: higher = more invited barbers caught before duplicate signup
rows.note: Steady-state settling toward ~27%, lower than Friday's 35.1% — early NEW window had more invited-barber backlog

rows.definition: Indies whose email matches a Rental/Commission account — likely accidental duplicate signups
rows.note: Pre-experiment baseline ~12.7/wk → experiment ~4.5/wk. Drop is bigger than the total-indies drop (37%) which suggests Flow B is specifically catching duplicates, not just riding organic decline.

verdict.headline: Flow B still wins on conversion + reduces accidental indie signups; one caveat (login_abandoned at email_entry)
verdict.body: Refresh confirms the core verdict. On 3x more data: SRM cleared (52/48), Flow B beats A on overall completion by +15pp (moderated from +31pp last week), forgot-password rate, welcome rate. Accidental indie signups dropped ~65% vs pre-experiment baseline. Caveats: Flow B has higher login_abandoned at the new email_entry step (Clarity will tell us if it's healthy filtering or friction), and per-exposure barber-via-signup-path rate is now solidly +3.4pp vs A — worth watching to understand if B is routing confused users to signup instead of login.

historicalComparison.headline: Refreshed 2026-05-11 with an 8-week pre-experiment baseline (more stable than the 3-week reading we used on Friday). Sharpest signal: accidental indies dropped ~65% vs the 8wk baseline.
historicalComparison.statusNote: IDENTIFIES table has 90+ days of history. Pre-experiment baseline is now an 8-week window (Mar 9 - Apr 20) for stability. The 'accidental indie' block below is the closest in-Snowflake proxy to the metric Eric/Tristan actually care about (accidental duplicate signups), without waiting for CS. CS support ticket data is still pending Tristan + CS team. NOTE: the duplicate-email block uses Friday's pull — refresh query is at docs/HISTORICAL_QUERIES.sql Q1.

blocks.title: ★ Accidental indies · weekly (sharpest signal)
blocks.subtitle: Indies whose email ALSO belongs to a Rental/Commission account — strong proxy for 'accidental duplicate signups'. This is what Flow B should specifically reduce.

comparison.verdict: Strong signal sustained on refresh: accidental indies dropped ~65% vs an 8-week baseline (12.9/wk → 4.5/wk), and the accidental rate among indies dropped from ~70% to ~46%. The fact that accidentals dropped MORE than total indies (~37% drop) remains the core evidence that Flow B is specifically catching the duplicate case, not just riding organic decline. Note: the late-pre-experiment weeks (Apr 6-20) had especially high accidental rates (~85%) which may have been an anomalous run-up of invited-barber backlog right before launch.

blocks.caveat: Refreshed 2026-05-11. Method: take all 'new indies' (role='barber', final user_type stayed null), cross-check whether their email also exists for any Rental/Commission user_id. Match = likely accidental.

shopTenureBreakdown.title: Refinement · shop tenure of accidental indies (90d cohort)

rows.bucket: Same day or earlier than shop account
rows.label: Likely legit transition (had indie biz, then joined a shop)
Likely accidental (downloaded app much later, signed up wrong)

shopTenureBreakdown.takeaway: 50% of 'accidental indies' under my original definition created their indie account before or same day as the shop account. Those are MORE LIKELY legitimate transitions (indie barber later joins a shop) than accidents. The TRUE accidental cohort (indie created after shop invitation) is ~101 of 201 in 90 days. The drop signal still holds even if we narrow to this cohort, but the absolute baseline is roughly half of what the headline number suggests.

blocks.title: New indie signups · weekly (total volume)
blocks.subtitle: Indies (final user_type stayed NULL) first appearing in IDENTIFIES, weekly. Drop observed during experiment weeks but the inference 'this is Flow B preventing accidental signups' is NOT yet established — could be organic.

comparison.verdict: Total indies dropped ~48% vs an 8-week baseline. But total ≠ accidental — some of this drop could be organic decline (volume was already trending down pre-experiment). See the ★ Accidental indies block above for the sharper signal — that one drops more (~65%) and is the proxy for what Flow B is meant to reduce. CS data (pending Tristan) is the cleanest signal.

blocks.caveat: Pre-experiment had a mild declining trend (~5/week). Even after correcting for trend, the experiment-window indie volume is well below extrapolation.

blocks.title: Duplicate barber-account-per-email rate
blocks.subtitle: % of new barber USER_IDs whose email already belongs to another USER_ID. Most direct prevention signal Flow B should move.

comparison.verdict: Slight downward drift (~15% relative) but inside week-to-week noise. Wait for 4+ weeks of post-rollout to call. NOTE: not refreshed for May 11 yet — query saved at docs/HISTORICAL_QUERIES.sql Q1, can re-pull next session.

blocks.caveat: Older weeks under-count duplicates because we don't have history before the 90-day window. Compare only to weeks with ≥60 days of prior history (Apr 13+) for a fair read. Re-check after 4+ weeks of post-rollout data.

blocks.title: New barber breakdown · Rental / Commission / Indie weekly
blocks.subtitle: Total new barbers per week segmented by final user_type. The mix shifted slightly toward shop-employed during experiment weeks.

comparison.verdict: Indie share dropped from 3.4% to 2.05% — directional support for Flow B's effect. The ★ Accidental indies block above is the sharper version of this same signal.

blocks.title: Active users base · weekly IDENTIFIES
blocks.subtitle: Total distinct devices in IDENTIFIES per week. Important denominator: if user base shrank during experiment, signup drops could be 'fewer users' rather than 'Flow B working'.

comparison.verdict: User base actually GREW slightly during the experiment (+4%) — so the drops in indie signups and accidentals are NOT explained by 'fewer users'. The drop signals are real and probably understated.

blocks.caveat: Excludes 2026-05-11 partial week (only Monday morning). Note IDENTIFIES distinct USER_IDs (multi-device users) actually exceeds distinct ANONYMOUS_IDs — that's normal.

blocks.title: CS support tickets · 'delete my accidental indie signup' volume
blocks.subtitle: Real-world signal of accidental duplicate signups — Tristan's suggestion + what Eric most cares about.
blocks.pendingNote: Tristan is connecting with the CS team next week to pull volumes pre vs post 2026-04-30. Will incorporate when available.

actionItems: Add a sharper 'accidental indie' metric: query Snowflake for indies whose email also belongs to a Rental/Commission barber account — that subset is the actual target of Flow B (vs total indies which mixes legit and accidental). Will make the historical comparison much cleaner without waiting for CS.

Redefine the experiment's success metric. Current 'indie/signup_link reduction' is unmeasurable. Candidates: (a) total per-device login completion rate, already a positive guardrail, (b) reduction in barber-via-signup-path rate (currently flowA 6.6% / flowB 11.5% — direction is wrong if Flow B should reduce it), (c) instrument indie_self_onboarding entry to measure cross-channel.

Decide whether to act on the SRM. New devices post-100% split 59/41 — still mildly skewed but close. If we want clean 50/50, options: re-bucket all devices via a new flag/seed, or restrict analysis to post-100% NEW devices only.
Ship PR #4143 (signup_link_tapped scope leak) so it stops contaminating the data.
Get Clarity installed on iOS (CLARITY_IOS_PROMPT.md is ready to hand to Yurii's session).
resolvedThisSession: Indie identifier: role='barber' AND user_type IS NULL. Zero indies in the experiment population.
temp_password ≠ indie: all temp_password Flow B users are confirmed Rental/Commission shop barbers.
SRM 73/27 root cause: sticky LD assignment + gradual rollout. New post-100% devices split 59/41 (close to expected). Not a bucketing bug.
flowB 3% logged-in rate explained: iOS auth flow fires only at session start (cold start / sign-in / context-switch / temp deep link). flowB skews to new anonymous devices.
Indie north-star is unmeasurable in this experiment: indies have a separate self-onboarding flow (*_INDY_SELF_ONBOARDING events) that the iOS login experiment doesn't touch.
Flow A frustration tapping is real and persistent (12-24 taps/user) — not a legacy artifact, concentrated in 5-9 users per period.
temp_password steady-state on clean data is ~27% — the apparent 33.5% → 23% drop layered two effects: sample contamination + invited-barber backlog clearing.
SRM resolved on clean NEW data: 52/48 as of 2026-05-11 — confirms the 73/27 was sticky-bucket artifact, not a bucketing flaw.

Accidental indies (indies whose email matches a Rental/Commission user) dropped ~65% vs an 8-week pre-experiment baseline — sharpest in-Snowflake signal that Flow B is reducing duplicates specifically.
